planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Harbour Belle is a dwarf compact nandina reaching 2–3 feet — far smaller than standard nandina (6–8 feet) and better suited for low foundation plantings, border edges, and containers. Like all nandinas, it spreads slowly by underground runners to form a colony; remove unwanted stems to maintain compact clump form. In zone 9 it is essentially evergreen; winter cold produces the red-orange foliage color change that makes nandina ornamentally distinctive in cool months. Avoid planting where berries may be consumed by cedar waxwings in large quantities, as nandina berries contain cyanogenic compounds.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Harbour Belle adapts to a wide range of soils and is drought tolerant once established, while tolerating substantial shade better than most broadleaf evergreen shrubs; Harbour Belle is a dwarf compact selection of Heavenly Bamboo (Nandina domestica), producing the characteristic Nandina feature set — pinnately compound leaves on cane-like stems, soft red new growth, summer green foliage, and brilliant red-orange to burgundy winter coloring — at half the size of standard nandina. The combination of multi-season foliage interest (red spring growth, green summer, then red-orange to burgundy in winter cold), adaptability to both sun and significant shade, drought tolerance once established, and the compact form that fits scale requirements of most residential plantings has made nandina in general — and compact forms specifically — one of the most heavily planted ornamental shrubs in the Gulf South. Harbour Belle's small size makes it particularly useful for low-growing foundation plantings, border edges, and mass planting under low windows where standard nandina would overgrow the site within 5–6 years. The fine-textured foliage and airy growth habit contrast well with coarser broadleaf companions like gardenias, hollies, and loropetalums.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Nandina Compact Cultivar Guide
University of Georgia Extension — Nandina domestica for Southern Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Evergreen Shrubs for Gulf South Foundation Plantings

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Savannah is a female cultivar requiring a male Ilex opaca pollinator within 50–100 feet for berry production — any male American holly in the vicinity will suffice. Trees bear berries beginning at 3–5 years from planting. In zone 9, Savannah Holly is well-adapted and one of the most prolific berry producers among American holly cultivars. Allow ample space — Savannah grows into a substantial tree (30–45 feet) over decades.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, acidic to slightly acidic soil — American holly adapts to a wide range of conditions but produces the densest berry crops in full sun with consistent moisture; Savannah is one of the most prolific and ornamentally outstanding cultivars of American Holly (Ilex opaca), the native evergreen holly that defines the winter ornamental landscape of the American East and Gulf South. Selected for heavy, consistent berry production, Savannah bears abundant clusters of bright red berries along every branch by November that persist through winter — providing both winter ornamental interest (the classic evergreen holly-and-berry combination) and critical food resources for overwintering birds including robins, cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, and yellow-rumped warblers. American holly is the native counterpart to English holly (Ilex aquifolium), with dull rather than glossy leaves and a more open, pyramidal growth habit — less formal than English holly but fully hardy throughout the South and adapted to the heat and humidity that limits English holly below zone 6. Savannah's native provenance means complete adaptation to Gulf South soils, climate, and pest pressure without the cultural requirements or limitations of introduced hollies.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — American Holly Cultivar Selection Guide
North Carolina State University Extension — Ilex opaca Native Tree Profile
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Ilex opaca Native Holly

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Edward Goucher blooms continuously from spring through frost, producing successive flushes of the lavender-pink tubular flowers that are distinctly more colorful than white-flowered Abelia x grandiflora. Prune lightly after the first spring flowering flush to maintain shape and stimulate reblooming; avoid heavy pruning that removes flowering wood. In zone 9 this abelia is nearly evergreen, losing only a portion of its leaves in the coldest winters. It tolerates pruning into a formal hedge but looks best when allowed to develop its naturally graceful, arching habit.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Edward Goucher Abelia is drought-tolerant once established and blooms most freely in full to partial sun; Edward Goucher is a hybrid abelia cross between Abelia x grandiflora and Abelia schumannii, producing a medium-sized (4–5 foot), gracefully arching shrub with distinctly more colorful flowers than standard white-flowered abelia — the small, tubular flowers are lavender-pink to lilac-pink, creating a softer, more distinctly colored floral display that continues from late spring through frost. The semi-evergreen to nearly evergreen foliage has a refined, fine texture, and the new growth emerges with a bronze-red tint that transitions to dark green — adding foliage color interest beyond the continuous flower display. Abelia in general is one of the most butterfly-attractive landscape shrubs available in zone 9, and Edward Goucher's colored flowers enhance this quality — the lavender-pink flowers are particularly attractive to swallowtails, skippers, and sulphurs throughout the long bloom season. The combination of zone 9 adaptability, long bloom season, drought tolerance once established, and the graceful natural habit has made Edward Goucher one of the most classic and enduringly popular landscape shrubs for Gulf South gardens since its introduction.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Abelia x 'Edward Goucher' Plant Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Abelia Varieties for Southern Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Flowering Shrubs for Gulf South Landscapes

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups/suckers) after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Ice Cream banana is one of the more cold-tolerant banana varieties — the rhizomes survive frost to about 22°F if heavily mulched, and the plant resprouts vigorously from the rhizome in spring even when the above-ground pseudostem is killed. In zone 9 a plant that survives multiple seasons as a ratoon crop (resprouting from the surviving rhizome) will bloom and produce fruit within 9–12 months of the spring resprouting. Allow suckers to develop as replacement ratoon plants when the main pseudostem flowers, since banana plants fruit only once per pseudostem.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, very fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil with consistent moisture — banana requires abundant water, nutrients, and heat to produce fruit; Ice Cream banana (also called Blue Java) is a cold-tolerant banana cultivar notable for its distinctive blue-green to silvery-blue coloring on unripe fruits and the unusually rich, creamy flavor of ripe fruit described as reminiscent of vanilla ice cream — a flavor and texture quality that sets this cultivar apart from the standard Cavendish banana flavor most North Americans are accustomed to. The ripe fruits develop a pale yellow to creamy-white flesh with a particularly soft, smooth, melting texture and vanilla-banana flavor that is among the finest of all banana varieties for fresh eating. Ice Cream's relative cold tolerance compared to Cavendish and other commercial varieties (the rhizome surviving to approximately 22°F with mulching) makes it one of the most practical banana varieties for zone 9 Gulf South gardens where winter freezes regularly kill the above-ground pseudostem. In zone 9 the combination of the summer heat, humidity, and rain that banana requires, combined with the cold-hardiness that allows ratoon production from surviving rhizomes, makes Ice Cream banana one of the most rewarding tropical fruit plants available.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Banana Cultivar Selection for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Bananas for Gulf South Home Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Cold-Hardy Banana Varieties for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Radiation Lantana is one of the more cold-hardy lantana selections, surviving mild zone 9 winters as a semi-woody perennial that dies back to the ground in freezes and resprouts vigorously in spring. Prune hard in early spring to remove dead wood and stimulate fresh, heavily flowering new growth. In zone 9 established plants can reach 4–5 feet tall and wide by late summer, producing the most spectacular color display from July through November. Avoid excessive fertilization — lean, well-drained conditions produce more flowers and better drought tolerance than rich, consistently moist plantings.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Radiation Lantana blooms most freely and is most drought tolerant in lean, dry conditions; avoid overwatering and heavy fertilization; Radiation is one of the most consistently spectacular lantana cultivars for the Gulf South — producing large, rounded flower clusters in a brilliant three-color combination of orange, red, and yellow that creates an intense, warm-colored floral display from spring through first frost. The tricolor flower clusters display all three colors simultaneously within each head, with individual florets transitioning from yellow through orange to red as they age — creating the radiating, multi-tonal effect that gives the cultivar its name. Lantana's combination of extreme summer heat and drought tolerance, continuous blooming without deadheading, and exceptional butterfly attraction (lantana is one of the top butterfly nectar plants available, visited by virtually every butterfly species that occurs in zone 9) has made it one of the most widely planted summer ornamentals for Gulf South gardens. Radiation's upright, bushy habit and particularly vivid color combination make it one of the most impactful lantana cultivars for large-scale planting in hot, sunny, drought-prone locations where the heat that defeats most plants allows lantana to reach its full ornamental potential.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lantana camara Cultivar Guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Lantana for Texas Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Perennials for Gulf South
